title: ESMGain: Effective and Efficient Prediction of Mutation’s functional Effect via ESM2 Transfer Learning and robust Benchmarks

reviews.summary: This paper proposes ESM-Effect, a simple and efficient framework for protein functional effect prediction that fine-tunes a 35M-parameter ESM2 model with a regression head emphasizing mutation-position features and an optional wildtype reference. Through ablations, the authors argue that fine-tuning (including LoRA and partial unfreezing) consistently beats static embeddings, that larger ESM2 models do not yield downstream gains for per-protein DMS prediction, and that mutation-position heads outperform mean-embedding heads on several assays. ESM-Effect slightly but consistently outperforms the multimodal PreMode (which uses static PLM embeddings, AF2 structures, and MSAs) on nine DMS datasets, and the paper introduces a new metric, relative Binned-Mean Error (rBME), and an interval-based split protocol intended to better evaluate performance in rare, non-neutral regimes.
strengths: The inductive-bias regression head focusing on the mutation position (with or without the wildtype token) is a clear, interpretable design choice that is validated across multiple DMS.

The work challenges common assumptions: (i) simple partial fine-tuning or LoRA can match full fine-tuning on these tasks, and (ii) larger ESM2 encoders do not reliably help downstream per-protein DMS prediction.
The rBME metric and interval-based split proposal address recognized shortcomings of correlation-based evaluation and random splits that over-emphasize abundant neutral mutations.
Head-to-head comparison with PreMode on the same nine DMS and matched splits; ESM-Effect slightly surpasses PreMode on most assays despite being sequence-only.
Ablations across fine-tuning strategies (LoRA vs full vs partial), model sizes, and head architectures, with qualitative inspection (prediction vs ground-truth scatter) to contextualize correlations.
Honest reporting of mixed transferability and a targeted interval-split study on SNCA, exposing IDR-related failure modes.
The problem motivation (functional effect vs pathogenicity) is well articulated, and the limitations of generalist predictors for bidirectional functional effects are clearly discussed.
The architecture diagrams and plot-based analyses (scatter, residuals, BME/rBME illustrations) help interpret model behavior beyond a single summary metric.
Demonstrating that a lightweight, sequence-only fine-tuned PLM can edge out a more complex multimodal method is surprising and practically relevant.
The benchmarking proposals (interval splits and rBME) have potential to influence more realistic evaluation practices in the field.
weaknesses: The core modeling contribution is modest—mutation-position heads and simple partial fine-tuning have been explored in prior works; the novelty is primarily empirical synthesis and careful ablation rather than a new algorithmic idea.

The evidence that LoRA ≈ full fine-tuning and that scaling laws “do not transfer” is based on a limited set of assays/settings and may not generalize; claims feel stronger than the presented scope warrants.
The rBME formulation divides by |y|, which may overweight near-zero bins unless epsilon is carefully chosen; a more thorough analysis of metric sensitivity and design alternatives is needed.
Limited transferability experiments (only SNCA) and no systematic evaluation across multiple IDR-heavy proteins to substantiate generalization claims.

rBME is only qualitatively motivated with a couple of examples; no comprehensive cross-model comparison or statistical tests demonstrate that rBME better ranks models in practice than Spearman across assays.
Few details on hyperparameters, regularization, and early stopping; some starred results use only 3/5 seeds, and significance intervals are not consistently reported.
Data and code availability are not clearly stated; the proposed benchmarking framework would benefit from public splits, scripts, and evaluation code.
Some figures/tables and captions appear to contain artifacts from PDF extraction, and a few sections are difficult to parse (e.g., Figure numbering, repeated lines).
The exact value for epsilon in rBME and binning specifics (e.g., how to handle empty bins) are not specified, which affects reproducibility and interpretability.

The paper could more fully situate itself relative to: (i) NLR fine-tuning (Lafita et al., 2024) which also emphasizes full end-to-end adaptation to DMS; (ii) structure- and retrieval-informed methods like Tranception/TranceptEVE and GEMME; (iii) few-shot test-time-training methods (PRIMO) that address distribution shift; and (iv) recent analyses of structure usage and IDRs in zero-shot fitness prediction.
The contrast between this paper’s finding that partial FT/LoRA suffice and Lafita et al.’s observation that full fine-tuning yields the best cross-protein gains deserves deeper discussion.
reviews.questions:
1. How exactly are epsilon, number of bins, and bin edges chosen for rBME? How do results vary with these choices, and how are empty/low-count bins handled?
2. Did you re-train PreMode or rely on reported results? If the latter, can you confirm identical preprocessing (e.g., score normalization, isoforms) and splits to ensure fairness?
3. What LoRA ranks and hyperparameters were used, and how sensitive are the LoRA ≈ full-FT results to these settings? Can you report parameter counts trained in each configuration?
4. Can you provide performance vs training set size curves to quantify data efficiency and elucidate when mean-based heads might suffice vs when mutation-position heads are essential?
5. Do your findings about model size and LoRA generalize when you move from per-protein fine-tuning to cross-protein training (e.g., mixing multiple DMSs), as in NLR?
6. Will you release code, trained weights, exact train/validation/test splits, and a reference implementation of rBME to enable adoption of your benchmarking framework?
7. For the interval-based splits, can you extend the analysis to multiple proteins and report aggregate statistics, particularly separating structured vs intrinsically disordered regions?
reviews.assessment: This is a solid empirical paper that makes a useful contribution to functional effect prediction with a minimal, interpretable, and efficient fine-tuning framework that matches or outperforms a more complex multimodal baseline across nine DMS assays. The ablation studies are thoughtfully designed and yield several practical insights (fine-tuning beats static embeddings; mutation-position heads are strong; partial fine-tuning/LoRA can suffice for per-protein DMS). The proposed evaluation ideas—interval splits and rBME—address real pain points in current benchmarking. The main limitations are modest methodological novelty, somewhat overgeneralized claims about scaling and LoRA vs full fine-tuning given limited scope, and incomplete validation and specification of the new rBME metric. The presentation suffers slightly from PDF artifacts, and broader related work coverage and reproducibility details could be improved. Overall, I view this as a valuable, practitioner-friendly contribution; with stronger metric analysis, broader transfer evaluation, and code/split release, it would merit publication. I lean toward a weak accept.
